title: Using Dictate in Microsoft Word
subtitle: Student Guide
purpose: Help students use Microsoft Word Dictate to speak ideas into a document.

introduction.heading: Introduction to Microsoft Word Dictate
introduction.whatIsIt: Dictate is a Microsoft Word feature that lets you speak your ideas out loud and have them typed into a document.
introduction.whyUseIt: It can help you get ideas down quickly, reduce typing fatigue, and focus on your thoughts before editing your work.
introduction.imagePlaceholder: Microsoft Word Home tab with the Dictate button highlighted
introduction.imageDescription: Microsoft Word open on a blank document with the Dictate microphone button highlighted on the Home tab.

whatYouNeed: A device with Microsoft Word installed or available online
gettingStarted.beforeYouBegin: Check that your microphone is connected and working. Close other apps that may be using your microphone.
gettingStarted.tip: Using headphones with a microphone can improve accuracy and reduce background noise.

steps.title: Open Microsoft Word
instructions: Open Microsoft Word on your device.
steps.tip: Have your document topic or notes nearby before you start dictating.
steps.imageDescription: Microsoft Word start screen showing recent documents and the option to open a blank document.

steps.title: Open a blank document
instructions: Select Blank document from the start screen.
steps.imageDescription: The Microsoft Word start screen with the Blank document option highlighted.

helpfulTips.whyItHelps: Helps Dictate recognise your words more accurately.
Reduces background noise and transcription errors.

troubleshooting.whatToTry: Check your internet connection and confirm that Word is up to date.
Check microphone access and make sure the microphone is selected.

faqs.question: Do I need to type at all?
faqs.answer: You can use Dictate for drafting, but you should still review and edit your work.
